titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Paul Rand Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Rachel Mihalko

publicationstmt.publisher: Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday November 8, 2023 at 00:00
Revised to include information about reprocessing, beginning July 2022.

corpname.part: Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections

did.abstract: The Paul Rand papers consist of sketches and drawings, artwork, proofs, mechanical art, paste-ups, source material, book jackets, realia, photographs and negatives, textiles, and posters documenting the career of graphic designer Paul Rand. It also contains material documenting Rand's artistic process and professional relationships, including correspondence, teaching materials, published books, newspaper clippings, and research files.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Marion Swannie Rand, 1999, 2000, 2001, 2012; transferred from Manuscripts and Archives to Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library, 2014.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is arranged into seven series: I. Art direction and graphic design work; II. Correspondence; III. Professional papers; IV. Biographical materials; V. Collected and source material; VI. Photographic slides; VII. Publicity and clippings.

bioghist.head: Biography
bioghist.p: Paul Rand was an American graphic designer whose work included art direction, corporate identity systems and logos, book jacket and magazine cover design, and advertising design. He was born as Peretz Rosenbaum on August 15, 1914 in Brooklyn, NY, to Isidore and Lena Hecht Rosenbaum. He had an identical twin brother, Philip (Fishel), and an older sister named Ruth. From 1937 to 1946 he was married to Harriet Wallace. In 1949 Rand married Anne Binkley (1918-2012), with whom he had one child. His third wife was Marion Swannie (1924-2018), who headed the graphic design department at IBM.

Rand studied art at Pratt Institute nights while in Haaren High school, from there earning a scholarship to Parsons School of Design. Later he studied under George Grosz at the Art Student League. His design career began in the 1930s as an apprentice in the design studio of George Switzer. The 1930s were also a time a growing anti-semitism, and in 1937 he changed his surname from Rosenbaum to Rand. From 1936-1941 he worked for Esquire-Coronet, at first creating layouts for Apparel Arts Magazine before becoming Esquire Magazine's art director. He quickly established himself as a creative, devoted designer, and an early practitioner of modernizing advertising design. During this time he was also producing politically charged covers for the anti-fascist publication Direction from 1938 to 1945. By 1941, Rand assumed the position of art director for William H. Weintraub Advertising Agency, a firm opened by one of Rand's former colleagues at Esquire-Coronet. Through this agency, Rand designed distinctive advertisements and developed his design style further through clients like Coronet Brandy, Schenley Liquors, Kaiser-Frazer, Stafford Fabrics, and Disney Hats. These advertisements introduced visual identities to the brands such as the Stafford Stallion and Coronet Brandy's Snifter Man.

While he was at William Weintraub, Rand also worked as a freelance designer to produce magazine covers, packages, and textiles. He began designing book jackets in the 1940s through 1964, chiefly for Alfred A. Knopf, Inc. and Bollingen series by Pantheon Books. In addition to book jackets, he illustrated four children's books between 1956 and 1970 with his second wife, Anne Binkley (as Paul and Ann Rand). Anne E. Ozbekhan, née Binkley, was an American architect trained under Mies van der Rohe, and in 1953 Paul and Anne designed and built their home in Weston, CT.

Rand began instructing graphic design at New York Laboratory School in 1939, then Cooper Union in 1942, and continued to teach at Pratt Institute in 1946. He was appointed professor at Yale University School of Art and Architecture beginning in 1956 until his retirement in 1993. He became professor emeritus in 1993, although he continued teaching Yale's Summer Program in Brissago, Switzerland through 1996.

In 1956 Eliot Noyes, architect and chief design consultant for IBM, named Rand for the redesign of IBM's corporate design program. In the same year he updated the IBM logotype, and maintained a robust design program until stepping down in 1991. Throughout his consultant work at IBM, Rand also freelanced as a graphic design consultant for other major corporations. He implemented design programs at Westinghouse Electric Corporation starting in 1959, and Cummins Engine Company in 1961. Some of the most well-known logos and trademarks created by Rand from the 1950s through the early 1990s include UPS, ABC, NeXT Computers, Education First, Enron, and Yale University Press.

In addition to his work as a designer, Rand was also a prolific author and lecturer on the topic of design. He wrote several books and articles on design, including Thoughts on Design , A Designer's Art , Design, Form, and Chaos , and From Lascaux to Brooklyn . His honors include an honorary degree of Master of Literature from Yale University, the title of Royal Industrial Designer by the Royal Academy, London, he was awarded the first Florence Prize, and was inducted into the Art Directors Hall of Fame of the New York Art Director's Club. He was also awarded medals from The American Institute of Graphic Arts (AIGA), the New York Art Directors Club, and Type Directors Club, among numerous others evidenced in this collection. Throughout his career he was a member of Alliance Graphique Internationale, Paris, the Art Directors Club of New York, and Industrial Designers of Society of America.
Rand died on November 26, 1996 in Norwalk, Connecticut.

The Graphic Art of Paul Rand, Pratt Gallery
Reinhold Brown Gallery, New York City
William Patterson College of New Jersey (Ben Shahn Gallery)
Paul Rand: A Designer's Art, Design Gallery
School of Visual Arts, Paul Rand: Master's Series
The Graphic Art of Paul Rand, University of Hartford

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Paul Rand papers (AOB 126). Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: In 2023 this collection was reprocessed to merge accessions the library received at different times. The papers were previously used and cited from a basic inventory compiled as accessions were received. The former call number was MS 1745.

Accessions were previously grouped as series and were classified under the numbers Original accession (1999-M-143), 2000-M-133, 2000-M-136, 2002-M-051, and 2013-M-031. Further processing notes at the series level indicate where to locate material from previous arrangement.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Paul Rand Library, Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Paul Rand papers consist of process materials, correspondence, writings, teaching records, publicity, and realia documenting the career of graphic designer Paul Rand. Materials that were generated in Rand's creative process include paintings, sketches, galley proofs, paste-ups, mechanicals, slide film, negatives, and photographic prints. Corporate client files usually contain early iterations of logos through sketches and paste-ups, along with research materials, proposals or related correspondence, and, often, a finished logo presentation book. The papers also consist of printed materials, including corporate design guideline books, pamphlets, brochures, posters, packaging, and advertisements. Other design work includes samples of book jacket designs and cover art for journals and magazines.

Personal and professional correspondence is also represented in the collection, including letters and notes from clients, colleagues and design collaborators, and friends. Additionally, there are manuscripts, handwritten notes, proofs, and published works by Rand, as well as photographs and slides documenting his work, personal life, and travels.

Other items of note in the collection include teaching materials, such as lectures, course outlines, design exercises, and student work, from his time at Cooper Union, Pratt Institute, and Yale University. Yale University files also contain sketches and proofs for the Yale University Press logo Rand designed in 1985, and School of Art recruitment posters, both process materials and final printed versions.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright has been assigned to the Trustee of the Paul Rand Charitable Trust by the creator of this collection for materials they have authored or otherwise produced. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

bibliography.head: Monographs by Paul Rand
bibliography.bibref: Rand, Paul. Thoughts on Design . New York: Wittenborn and Company, 1947. Catalog record.
Rand, Paul. Paul Rand: A Designer's Art . New Haven: Yale University Press, 1985. Catalog record.
Rand, Paul. Design, Form, and Chaos . New Haven: Yale University Press, 1993. Catalog record.
Rand, Paul. From Lascaux to Berlin . New Haven: Yale University Press, 1996. Catalog record.

bibliography.head: Source used
bibliography.bibref: Heller, Steven. Paul Rand . London: Phaidon Press, 1999. Catalog record.

corpname.part: Yale University. School of Art. Graphic Design Program

did.unittitle: Series I: Art direction and graphic design work

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into six subseries: Corporate design, logos and brand identities; Advertising design; Book jackets; Magazine, periodical, and journal covers; Posters; Design portfolios. Files are arranged alphabetically by client within each grouping unless otherwise noted.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: These files were previously referenced as "job bags" and housed in oversize paper envelopes, presumably as they were originally maintained. In order to facilitate ease of use, access, and long term preservation, materials were rehoused into size-appropriate archival boxes and folders. Oversize files were separated into flat boxes during processing and are reflected as such in the finding aid. Envelopes were photocopied on archival paper and filed with contents to preserve Rand's original title information, notes, and sometimes date.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I consists of working files generated in the production of graphic design. The majority of materials include drawings and sketches, comprehensive layouts ("comps"), research, mechanicals, galley proofs and mock-ups, paste-ups, camera-ready artwork, Pantone swatches, and printed material. Final presentation formats include signage, flyers, brochures, posters, billboards, advertisements, symbols and icons, packaging, book and magazine covers, and novelty items.
Also included are related design proposals, business records, correspondence, contracts, and other background information regarding trademarks and corporate identity.

did.unittitle: Corporate design, logos, and brand identities

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The materials in this series document work created in the process of developing brand identities, including logos, trademarks, information design, and other corporate graphic design. Project files may include technical drawings, artwork, sketches, correspondence, research and background information, packaging, and realia.

Sketches and drawings represent the logo design process from a client proposal to a finished visual identity. Many client files include sketches on any available blank surface, including napkins, post-its, and hotel notepads, and feature hand-drawn reworkings of design elements. Logo development materials also may include paintings and mechanical art or collages.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by client.
Project files were originally separate from final products. Related files were combined by the archivist to facilitate comparison and easier access to like materials.

did.unittitle: Type Talks cover and poster, "With the sense of sight, the idea communicates the emotion"—Alfred North Whitehead

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Proofs, correspondence, interview with Mario Rampone of PDR, and final printed versions of Type Talks .

processinfo.p: Also referred to as English First.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sketches, artwork, poster design, proofs, and logo presentation book for Education First's logo and other promotional materials for various programs.

title.part: A proposal to Ford Motor Company for a new House Mark

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: File includes logo presentation book draft and sketches.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes background correspondence and hand-drawn logo sketches.

title.part: A Logotype for IIT Research Institute

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Files originally in numbered job bags were rearranged by the archivist to combine like material. Groupings are organized by project type, and arranged chronologically within each. Original filing contained overlapping projects that were moved to related files but may still exist in some places. This especially applies to files related to copiers/printers and typewriters/dictation machines, which were more difficult to distinguish and potentially shifted from the order maintained by Rand.
Nameplate files contain several different types of projects but include only designs for nameplates. The nameplates were originally grouped separately and that order was retained.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: IBM posters and other final versions of design work are also in subseries: Posters and Design portfolios.
Documentation of IBM Gallery exhibitions are filed in Series III with other exhibition-related materials. Series III

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paul Rand began working with IBM in 1956 and redesigned the existing logo the same year. His first iteration was a solid or outlined set of letterforms in 1956, followed by the addition of the 13-bar logo with stripes in 1967. The introduction of the 8-bar stripe logo was introduced in 1972 to enhance flexibility in various uses of the logo. Rand created the rebus design "eye-bee-M" in 1981 as part of a "Think" campaign.

This grouping comprises process materials created while Rand worked as a consultant for the IBM Design Program. Files may include preliminary sketches, correspondence, paste-ups, galley proofs, transparencies, artwork, collages, packaging specifications, and working drawings related to IBM's corporate identity system. Rand was responsible for implementing design guidelines through corporate booklets, as well as stationery, posters, press releases, "Think" magazine, novelties, packaging, and signs. He also contibuted to Product Design Centers, wayfinding and instructional content, subsidiary logos, annual report and information design, and promotional material for new products and software releases. He continued design work for IBM into the early 1990s.

Original envelopes with Rand's assigned titles and filing numbers were photocopied onto archival paper and filed with materials to retain context. Numbering is indicated at the front of each title, except for materials that were originally unnumbered.

Titles were created by the archivist to capture actual contents and are described as observed, although some titles were retained from the original envelope. Date information was retrieved from documents in each file as they were available.

arrangement.p: Groupings of related material are arranged in loose chronological order for products and equipment. Internal design work is generally arranged by job bag number, as described in file titles.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Access to student and personnel files is prohibited for 75 years or life plus five years (whichever is longer).

did.unittitle: UCLA Extension cover design Fall 1994, 75th anniversary
UCLA Extension Spring 1997, cover design featuring Paul Rand

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence and proofs regarding design for Harry Jack Gray Center dedication event

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 50th Anniversary of the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising event design proofs and correspondence

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paul Rand was commissioned to create a visual identity for Westinghouse Electric in 1959. This grouping represents his artistic process and development of the circle W logo as well as his implementation of the corporate design program with a Graphics Identification Manual and guidelines. It contains working files, sketches, artwork, galley proofs, correspondence, and packaging. It also includes annual reports, advertisements, textiles, and stationery samples.
Documentation and samples of the Westinghouse Gothic typeface, created by Rand, is also included in these files.
Also present are photographs, press releases, and planning documents from the development of a computer-controlled sign constructed for the Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania building.

did.unittitle: Corporate Design Center employee memento book
Logo design, original sketches and mechanical art
President's memorandum establishing Corporate Design Center
Corporate sign systems and reference sheet for sign painting
Animated sequence sign for Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania Wesco building

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence, photographs, and press release regarding the construction of an outdoor building sign which lights up.

did.unittitle: Advertisements, Westinghouse Today! and Aspen News
Westinghouse Gothic alphabet, proof sheets and transparencies
Corporate Design Center, Operating and Strategic Programs
HR6161: An Act of Congress film poster, mechanicals and sketches

relatedmaterial.p: Rand designed the logo for Yale University Press in 1985. For materials related to his teaching career at Yale, see Series III, subseries Teaching.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Rand created jackets for this publisher between 1956 and 1964. It was led by Helen and Kurt Wolf with Jacques Schifrin, who have records in the correspondence files of this group.

The Archetypal World of Henry Moore, Erich Neumann
Change: 8 Lectures on the I Ching, Hellmut Wilhelm
Creative Intuition in Art and Poetry, Jacques Maritain
The Limits of Art, Huntington Cairns
The Gothic Cathedral, Otto von Simson
Literary Language and Its Public in Late Latin Antiquity and in the Middle Ages, Erich Auerbach
Pandora's Box, Dora and Erwin Panofsky
Prometheus: Archetypal Image of Human Existence, C. Kerényi
Eleusis: Archetypal Image of Mother and Daughter, C. Kerényi
Myths and Symbols in Indian Art and Civilization, Heinrich Zinmer
The Portrait in the Renaissance, John Pope-Hennessy
The Origins and History of Consciousness, Erich Neumann
The 'I' and Not-I', M. Esther Harding
The Myth of the Eternal Return, Mircea Eliade
Selected Works of Miguel de Unamuno

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes oversize samples of all titles with the addition of the following that are only available in an oversize print: Mudra: A Study of Symbolic Gestures in Japanese Buddhist Sculpture, E. Dale Saunders (1959); Jewish Symbols in the Greco-Roman Period, Erwin R. Goodenough (1965); On Quality in Art, Jakob Rosenberg (1967).

did.unittitle: The Future of an Illusion, Sigmund Freud
The Craft and Context of Translation, William Arrowsmith and Roger Shattuck
The Treasure of the Copper Scroll, John Marco Allegro
Of Time, Work, and Leisure, Sebastian de Grazia
The 20th Century Capitalist Revolution, A.A. Berle, Jr.
Problems of Contemporary Art no. 1: Form and Sense, Wolfgang Paalen
Origins of Modern Sculpture, W.R. Valentiner
The Dada Painters and Poets: An Anthology (Documents of Modern Art, Vol. 8), Robert Motherwell

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Physical copies of published books featuring the book jackets designed by Paul Rand. All book jackets in these boxes are also in the foldered book jacket files before this.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Also see: series II: Professional correspondence, Marguerite Tjader Harris, 1938–1942, Box: 50 Folder: 7

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Direction was an anti-fascist publication from 1937 to 1945 founded and edited by Marguerite Tjader Harris.

did.unittitle: Eagle design for centennial booklet, from [Ivan] Chermayeff
A Factual Study of Readers and Market
Hamburg-American Line: Pleasure Cruises to the West Indes
The Klee Foundation, Paintings, Drawings, and Prints by Paul Klee
Museum of Modern Art 20th anniversary publication: Modern Art in Your Life
New York World's Fair brochure and cover sample

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Compiled for P/M Magazine by Laboratory School of Industrial Design

title.part: Operation Peace: A Photographic Story of the 1957-1958 Mediterranean Cruise of the USS Randolph

did.unittitle: Type Directors Club, A Twenty Four Page Book
20th Century Art from the Arensberg Collection, School of the Art Institute of Chicago exhibition poster
Earth Day New York, Earth Fair '95

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poster design to celebrate the 25th anniversary of Earth Day, with an exhibition organized by the Pushpin Group in Grand Central Terminal. Files include mechanical designs, sketches, and correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sources and Resources of 20th Century Design poster (1966) and The Prepared Professional (1982).

did.unittitle: The House in the Museum Garden by Marcel Breuer
Portrait of Picasso catalogue cover design
Poster exhibition for the National Foundation for Infantile Paralysis, polio sketch
No Way Out , 20th Century Fox billboard photographs
No Way Out 24-sheet billboard screenprint proofs
Richard Sapper exhibition poster design proofs
SOS Kinderdorf poster design, correspondence and mechanicals

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Proofs and press photograph.

did.unittitle: Vietnam Moratorium Committee, death mask anti-war poster
The Art Directors Club of New York and Cincinnati

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3rd International Exhibition call for entries, Cincinnati Art Director's Club Paul Rand Exhibition poster, and poster for Design 63.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains posters created from children's book illustrations: an ice cream cone and heart from Sparkle and Spin; animals from Listen! Listen!; and an oil lamp and text design from I Know a Lot of Things.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poetics of Music by Igor Stravinsky, Prejudices by H.L. Mencken, and The Dada Painters and Poets: An Anthology (Documents of Modern Art, Vol. 8) by Robert Motherwell.

did.unittitle: The Splendor of Dresden, National Gallery of Art

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sources and Resources of 20th Century Design poster (1966) and The Prepared Professional (1982).

did.unittitle: "Man is distinguished from all other creatures by the faculty of laughter." Joseph Addison, 1712. [laugh poster]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Trademark posters and poster with quote "What would life be if we had no courage to attempt anything?" Vincent van Gogh

did.unittitle: Quality statement for IBM and Westinghouse
Tokyo Communication Arts and Osaka Communication Arts
U.S. Department of the Interior, National Park Service

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Benjamin Franklin 200 Year Celebration, Boston National Historical Park, and Minute Man National Historical Park.

did.unittitle: University of Hartford, University Center Dedication

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: UCLA Extension Winter Quarter, Summer Sessions 1993, and UCLA 75th anniversary.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Westinghouse advertisement and New York Times 1967 spread.

did.unittitle: Yale School of Art Graduate Program

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Original binders were dismantled and discarded for preservation purposes. Portfolio pages were moved into archival folders and original order was retained.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into two subseries: Professional Correspondence and Personal Correspondence. Arranged alphbetically by correspondent, followed by unidentified material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II contains Paul Rand's correspondence with clients, professionals in the arts, other designers, family, and friends. It documents his personal and professional relationships, which were closely intertwined. Many letters in this series are from artists, architects, and designers that also collaborated with Rand.
This series also contains correspondence from employees of IBM, Westinghouse, Cummins Engine Company, and other corporations for whom Rand did consultation work.

Letters are chiefly incoming with some typescript responses from Rand. The general file includes incoming correspondence from friends and some fan mail. Letters from former students are filed in Series III in the teaching subseries with relevant institution. Many letters are addressed to both Rand and his wife, Marion.

Correspondence frequently contains extensive sketches, usually unrelated to the content of the letter. Sometimes sketches are related to projects completed in the same year as a dated letter, but some may not be associated with any specific project. These drawings on any available blank paper serve as a record of Rand's artistic practice and reworking of design ideas.

The correspondence was originally arranged in rough alphabetical order and was rearranged by the archivist more thoroughly. Correspondents with more than one letter were identified and moved into a separate folder. Unidentifiable and infrequent senders remained in the original general file.

Some professional correspondence was left in its original order in project files, consult Series I for a full record of Rand's business correspondence. Overlap with graphic design process materials may occur in places where an individual is affiliated with a client or organization listed in Series I.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes inquiries from institutions seeking to include Rand's design work in exhibitions, exhibition judging invitations, business matters, and design requests.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Files are in reverse chronological order.

did.unittitle: Graphis Press contributions to Walter Herdeg, including "Typography 1"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Professional opportunities including graphic design program evaluations, scholarship contributions, panel and seminar invitations.

did.unittitle: Requests for comments, articles, and appearances

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include other designers and friends, Rand's mother Lena Rosenbaum, and some business clients. The letters provide insight to Rand's design process, personal activities, and collaborations with other artists.

did.unittitle: Letters commenting on Paul Rand's work

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Individuals with whom Rand had correspondence but were filed with the group "Research file on design" were relocated to this group to more accurately reflect the description of the materials. Some of these files contain design samples that served as inspiration or research. For simplicity and retention of original order within the file, these materials remained with the related correspondence.

did.unittitle: Eames, Charles and Ray correspondence and research file
Eames, Charles and Ray exhibition materials for The World of Franklin and Jefferson

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes an abbreviated portfolio of Rand's work, correspondence with Marion Rand, sketches, and letterhead for Doug Evans + Partners.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: File includes letters to and from Rand's mother while she lived in Israel.

did.unittitle: Tress, Maria, correspondence regarding Wilhelm Deffke

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series III is organized into six subseries: Exhibitions; Honors and awards; Memberships; Teaching; Writings; and other professional papers.

Subseries were created by the archivist to combine related material the library received in separate shipments. Original chronological arrangement was retained within correspondence files and original titles were retained when descriptions reflected the contents of the files. Titles and new files were created by the archivist to group like material and identify loose papers.
Files are arranged chronologically unless otherwise noted.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III consists of Paul Rand's exhibition files, teaching activities and outlines, writings and publications, and other records generated from the design field, including awards, honors, professional memberships, and financial records.
Also included are drafts of essays written by Rand without an associated publication, although many of the concepts appear elsewhere in Rand's published books.

This series duplicates visual material in Series I and are not dated in this grouping as it could not be determined when reproduced negatives were created. This applies to negatives and photographs of finished works, reproductions used for published books, trademark and typography examples, and advertising proofs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Not all exhibitions with Paul Rand's work included are represented in this group.

did.unittitle: Requests from schools and museums for exhibits and samples
Exhibition judging, International Poster Triennial in Toyama, Japan

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This grouping is arranged chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Exhibition files may include photographs, gallery floorplans, checklists, printed materials, and correspondence.

did.unittitle: "The Graphic Art of Paul Rand," IBM Gallery Retrospective

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes press release, poster, visitor feedback, photographs, checklists, loan requests

did.unittitle: School of Visual Arts, "Masters Series: Paul Rand"
University of Hartford, "The Graphic Art of Paul Rand"
Ginza Graphic Gallery, Tokyo, "A Paul Rand Exhibition"
Cincinnati Art Directors Club, University of Cincinnati, Paul Rand exhibition poster
Fashion Institute of Technology, Ogilvy and Mather videotape of Paul Rand opening
Traveling exhibitions: correspondence and installation instructions
Loan exhibition from National Collection of Fine Arts, Smithsonian, "Portrait of America: 1865-1915"
"Leonardo da Vinci" traveling exhibition, design proofs and photographs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes diplomas, honorary degrees, certificates, and correspondence related to honors and awards.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: General file consists of correspondence relating to honors, memberships, appointments, and occasionally a certificate or diploma.

did.unittitle: Honorary degree, School of Visual Arts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Title retained from original file. Contains correspondence mainly with Marion Rand and others.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Files are arranged alphabetically by name of organization.

did.unittitle: American Center for Design, member directory
Society of Typographic Arts (STA) Journal and member list

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Access to student files may be prohibited for 75 years or life plus five years (whichever is longer).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Files are arranged alphabetically by name of institution.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Faculty appointment correspondence, artwork, and teaching exercises.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains correspondence related to his teaching appointment for Advertising Design, including syllabi for Display Design IIa (Advertising Design), and exercises for design problems.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes lesson plans and student work related to the parcheesi problem (Graphics 120), proportion systems, and selected design examples.

did.unittitle: Invitations for speaking, seminars, and symposia
School requests for lectures and consultations
Yale School of Art Graduate Studio in Graphic Design teaching materials

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Design and the Play Instinct"

did.unittitle: Yale Summer Program in Graphic Design, Brissago, Switzerland
Syllabi, schedules, Léger problem, book lists

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Access to student files is prohibited for 75 years or life plus five years (whichever is longer).

did.unittitle: Class thank you cards and photographs
Léger projects, student work and class lists

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Access to student files is prohibited for 75 years or life plus five years (whichever is longer).

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Also see: Series I, Wittenborn and Company correspondence.

did.unittitle: Thoughts on Design, revised edition blank copy
Book jacket final samples for A Designer's Art and Thoughts on Design

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many letters overlap with correspondents in other series.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence with editors from Yale Press with feedback, an addendum, and comments from readers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence and financial documentation related to titles published with Yale University Press.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These are notes on themes that appear in published books but there is no specific title mentioned. Descriptive information was retained when available.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drafted notes for concepts used in later writings, such as grid systems, Cezanne, space, perspective, and art historical references.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Examples of Chinese and Muslim calligraphy, corporate brochures, and some correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typed manuscript notes for an unidentified publication, including essays and notes to an introduction and themes such as perspective, grids, space, seeing, math, and other art historical references.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written by Anne Binkley (published under Ann Rand), illustrated by Paul Rand.

title.part: I Know a Lot of Things

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence, invoices, contracts, reprint information, and royalty statements.

did.unittitle: I Know a Lot of Things, Japanese edition correspondence and financials
A Critique of the 50 Books, Bookbinding and Book Production
Modern and Traditional Typography in America, Penrose Annual
Black in the Visual Arts, Graphic Forms, Harvard University Press
The Story of a Symbol, Type Talks
The Art of the Poster, Posters, Rheinhold Publishing
Modern Typography in the Modern World, Typographica 5
The Trademark as an Illustrative Device, Trademark Design, Theobold
Seven Designers Look at Trademark Design, Theobold
Interview for Type Talks, Advertising Typographers Association of America, number 84
The Good Old NEUE Typografie, Typography USA, Type Directors Club
How Can the Designer and Management Bridge Their Gap of Understanding?, "Print" panel
The Art of the Package, Print
Advertisement: Ad Vivum or Ad Hominem?, Daedalus, Journal of American of Arts & Sciences
Our Biggest Threat is Conformity, Printers Ink
The Trademarks of Paul Rand: A Selection, Wittenborn
Design and the Play Instinct, "The Education of Vision" Braziller
Jan Tschichold: The New Typography, Print magazine
Unsung Heroes, Art Directors Club newsletter volume 2, number 2
Design Quarterly 123: A Paul Rand Miscellany, drafts and final
Good Design is Good Will, AIGA Journal
Art Center College of Design Review 5, volume 2 number 1, including panel transcript

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: File contains correspondence and full transcript.

did.unittitle: Childhood Fantasies, book by Champion Papers featuring Paul Rand
"Object Lessons: A Review of the Evolution of Useful Things" by Henry Petroski," article for The New Criterion
"Failure by Design" NY Times op-ed notes, drafts, and booklet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also working title "Corporate Design Dilemma"

did.unittitle: Columbus, Indiana, A Look at Architecture 7th edition for Marion Rand

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries includes additional material related to Rand's professional activities.

did.unittitle: Notebook containing finances, logo sketches, and design notes
Business label and stamp, blank client cards
National Film Board of Canada project
Gottschalk + Ash International design research and correspondence

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: PDR, IBM, Yale, University of Hartford, posters; 4x5 and 8x10

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series IV is arranged chronologically, followed by undated material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV contains school diplomas, photographs of Paul Rand and his Weston, CT home, ephemera, greeting cards, sketches and notes, and publicity materials.

did.unittitle: Haaren High school diploma and course completion certificate from Pratt Institute
Astrological charts and analysis from Ferdinand Ostertag

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Other correspondence in the collection identifies "Ferdi" as the name of a friend who compiles astrological charts.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains a selection of newsletters and other publications, letters, examples of information design, and cover designs. These were foldered together without a descriptive title. Files remain in the order they were received because the archivist could not determine context.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Notes may be related to concepts that appear in manuscript drafts and published books. Sketches include skull, Yale "Y", a bird, Polaroid, liberty bell and sihouettes of faces, KLM, Gazette, and Mr. Toy. A group of sketches and collage works for IBM retained the original organization within a folder and include packaging specs and "PAGE" design.

did.unittitle: Unidentified artwork [Green candelabra with red background]; [Microphone with American flag pattern on blue background with white star]
Series V: Collected and source material

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series V is organized in three subseries: Manuscripts and documents; Photographs; and Resource file on design. Files are arranged alphabetically by content.
Materials in this series were originally sorted together and were rearranged within the existing groups to alphabetical order.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series V comprises materials used for reference and research. It includes files on individual designers, source material, photograms, and some of Rand's own work. Visual themes from this series appear in Rand's design work, including the abacus photogram used for his Thoughts on Design book cover.
It also contains transparencies for grids and rulers, typeface samples, and color swatches from Rand's design studio that were likely used in creating artwork.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes files that were grouped together by the creator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Examples of design work similar to Paul Rand's published works.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The original title for this file is "wall stuff," and likely refers to materials that formerly decorated the walls of Paul Rand's studio space. Files include transparencies for rulers and grid systems references, typeface samples, works by other designers, especially Fernand Léger, and various design elements used in Rand's work.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: File includes circuit board pattern used for IBM.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Title is retained from original description; contains weather patterns and other unidentifiable photographs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: File primarily includes photo proofs of perfume bottles.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: These files were originally in loose alphabetical order and were slightly rearranged to reflect a higher level of detail.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Consists of articles, clippings, exhibition catalogs, and printed material. Includes files on individuals, corporations, topics, genres, and geographic areas. Files in this grouping which previously included correspondence with other designers were moved to Series II: Correspondence.

Research files were likely collected by Rand and used as inspiration for his own design or as background design reference on clients' existing or historical brand identities. These files do not contain any of Rand's original work.

did.unittitle: Cassandre, A. M. booklets and advertisements
Expo 67, the Universal and International Exhibition of 1967 in Montreal, Canada
Garretto, Paolo article by Steven Heller for Print Magazine
Norman Bel Geddes studio assignment log

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes articles by date and by subject: mathematical games.

did.unittitle: Oversize records from files: painters, Polish design, and William Morris

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series VI retained its original order and is arranged alphabetically by client or project name.
Slides were originally housed in slide boxes and were rehoused for preservation purposes. Original titles were retained and are reflected in the file arrangement.
Some slides previously filed under "general" were identified and moved to related materials during processing.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series VI consists of 35mm color photographic slides of Paul Rand's projects, most of which also have process materials in Series I. Work that is only reproduced in this series include the following: some Esquire campaigns; Tri-Arts Press poster; Pratt Insitute design; and Paul Rand's paintings. It also includes research images created by other designers.

The slide boxes contained identifying labels for the following businesses/subject headings for which there were no associated slides: Adstar, Apparel Arts, Container Corporation, Coronet, El Producto – Lovera, Gentry, PDR, Stafford, Target, Tipton Lakes, and U.S. Department of the Interior. The labels were removed, but it is possible that images for these categories may be found in the general or unidentified slides (box 103).
Formerly titled Series Accession 2013-M-031.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series VII retained the original loosely chronological arrangement and is organized into two subseries: Writings by others and Publicity albums.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series VII consists of collected newspaper clippings, articles, and printed material related to Rand's design work.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Materials in this grouping retain the order in which they were received and contain printed versions of articles with minimal correspondence in some places. Overlap with clippings in publicity volumes may occur.

did.unittitle: "Paul Rand," by Percy Seitlin, American Artist
Paul Rand, Advertising Artist, by Percy Seitlin, Magazine of Art
"Paul Rand's Designs Exhibited at A-D Gallery" Publisher's Weekly article
"Art is like Digging Ditches" by Shana Ayer, PM Newspaper clipping
Paul Rand, by Georgine Oeri, Graphis
"What is Modern Typography?" by Eugene M. Ettenberg, American Printer
Variations on the Theme of Garamond, by E.M. Ettenberg, American Printer
Paul Rand, F.H.K. Henrion, Printed Advertising
Trademarks by Paul Rand, Portfolio #1 magazine
On Paul Rand by Y. Kamekura, Idea magazine
Paul Rand, by Peter Heemskerk, Graphik
A House to Live With, Esquire
The Paul Rand Legend by E.M. Ettenberg, American Artist
Ett Uus Att Leve Med, by Olle Eksell, Obsi
The El Producto Story by P.K. Thomajan
De ritade Sjalva Sitt Hus by Olle Eksell, Vecko-Journalen
The Pop Stays in the Bottle by Hal Stebbins, Printers' Ink
Journal of the American Institute of Graphic Arts (AIGA) #3
Paul Rand, Commercial Artist by Percy Seitlin for American Artist
How Paul Rand Presents Trademark Designs to Clients by Stanley Mason
Champion Papers, The Printing Salesman's Herald: Book 35, special issue on Paul Rand
IBM--Business and Education by Steve Heller
"The Graphic Genius of Paul Rand" by Jon Bowers in Connecticut's Finest magazine
"The Art of Graphic Design" exhibition, Virginia Museum of Fine Arts
UPS Big Idea magazine, " The Logo Maker" by Kathy Shovlin
Paul Rand, El Pionero, by Carlos Alberto Méndez Mosquera in Publicidad
Paul Rand's Laboratory: The Art of Book Jackets and Covers , by Steven Heller, Baseline

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Publicity albums were originally kept in binders that were removed before arriving at Haas Arts Library, presumably for ease of access and preservation purposes.
Clippings are annotated to indicate where Paul Rand is named and may have been acquired through a media monitoring service.
